titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Tano D'Amico Photographs and Papers
titlestmt.author: by Matthew Daniel Mason and Nora Soto

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This collection contains 70 published digital objects. And those objects are comprised of 196 digital files.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid written in English.

Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday June 6, 2024 at 03:02

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: In Italian.

did.abstract: The collection consists of photographic prints, contact prints, and negatives by Italian photojournalist Tano D'Amico documenting social movements, political events, and daily life in Italy and Palestine, as well as notebooks containing notes for D'Amico's lectures, essays, and books. Materials are dated from 1969 to 2021.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from L'Arengario Studio Bibliografico on the Frank Altschul Fund and Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2009.
Purchased from L'Arengario Studio Bibliografico on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2013.
Purchased from Tano D'Amico on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2017.
Purchased from Tano D'Amico on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2019.
Purchased from Tano D'Amico on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2020.
Purchased from Gaetano D'Amico on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2022.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into six series: I. 2009 Acquisition, 1976-1981. II. 2013 Acquisition, 1977. III. 2017 Acquisition, 1969-2007. IV. 2019 Acquisition, 1987-2002. V. 2020 Acquisition, circa 1980-2019. VI. 2022 Acquisition, circa 2010–2021.

bioghist.head: Tano D'Amico (1942–)
bioghist.p: Tano D'Amico is a prominent photographer of Italian social and political contestation of the 1970s. As lead photographer for the radical leftist newspaper Lotta Poetica , D'Amico chronicled street protests and confrontations with authorities culminating in the Movimento del '77 and the abduction and assassination of Aldo Moro in 1978. Later he traveled to Palestine to document the First and Second Intifadas, and he continues to document protests including the anti-globalization movement and, most recently, a series of rallies and marches in defense of the homeless in Rome. Since the late 1990s, D'Amico has delivered public lectures and published numerous books of essays on photography, and in recent years his work has been featured in exhibitions of artistic photography.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Tano D'Amico Photographs and Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections as they are acquired, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

Various acquisitions associated with the collection have not been merged and organized as a whole. Each acquisition is described separately in the contents list below, identified by a unique call number and titled according to month and year of acquisition.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.
Former call number: GEN MSS 1006. The 2013 accession was assigned the call number GEN MSS 1006; in April 2020, the acquisition transferred to GEN MSS 655.
Former collection title: From April 2020 to April 2021, this collection was previously titled Tano D'Amico Photographs of Events in Italy. It was revised to reflect newer acquisitions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection consists of photographic prints, contact prints, and negatives by Italian photojournalist Tano D'Amico documenting social movements, political events, and daily life in Italy and Palestine, as well as notebooks containing notes for D'Amico's lectures, essays, and books, which offer insight into his writing process.

D'Amico's photographs of Italy capture social and political unrest in Rome, Florence, Milan, Montalto di Castro, Sicily, and other parts of Southern Italy between 1969 and 2019. Images depict the autonomous student movement Movimento del '77, radical leftist organizations such as Lotta continua and Potere operaio, labor strikes, marches advocating for the rights of the poor and the homeless, and the mobilization of the Carabinieri. The collection also includes images of Romani (Roma) residents of squatter's settlements in Rome. Other photographs depict Palestinian Arabs during the first Intifada and the Al-Aqsa Intifada throughout Palestine, Gaza Strip, and the West Bank, as well as in refugee camps. Notable subjects in the collection include German artist Joseph Beuys; Italian political activists Antonio Negri, Paola Meo, and Félix Guattari; and Italian terrorist Antonio Lo Muscio, member of Nuclei armati proletari.

Material within this collection has been organized by acquisition reflecting the fact that the collection has been acquired in increments over time. Researchers should note that material within each acquisition overlaps with/or relates to material found in other acquisitions.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Tano D'Amico Photographs and Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographic prints, contact sheets, and negatives created by D'Amico that document social and political events in Italy, 1976-1981.

A group of images comprised of 40 negatives and one contact sheet documents attendees at the second congress of Lotta Continua in Rimini from October to November 1976 and includes portraits of Adriano Sofri and others.

Fifty-one photographic prints documents political and cultural turmoil surrounding the events of 1977 in Rome, Bologna, and Montalto di Castro, known as Movimento del '77. Images include demonstrations and police mobilizations. Several images also depict attendees at the Umbria Jazz Festival in July 1976.

A group of images comprised of three photographic prints, forty-seven negatives, and one contact sheet, documents the aftermath of the kidnap of Italian politician Aldo Moro on March 16, 1978, in Rome, including views of the abduction site, the body of one of his escorts, and the surrounding crowds. It consists of forty-seven negatives, one contact sheet, and three photographic prints.

A group of 17 photographic prints documents the arrests of political activists in Padua on April 7, 1979, and subsequent protests surrounding the arrests at the Sapienza University of Rome. Identified individuals include defendant Antonio Negri, his wife Paola Meo, and one of his supporters, Félix Guattari.

A group of images comprised of 282 negatives and eight contact sheets documents Joseph Beuys creating an art installation entitled Terremoto at the Palazzo Braschi in Rome, organized by the Lotta Continua newspaper on April 7, 1981. The photographs show Beuys at work and addressing a crowd during its unveiling.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under three headings: Photographs in Bologna, Photographs of Rome, and Photographs of Montalto di Castro and unidentified locations.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Negatives and contact sheets with images that document social and political events in Italy, 1977. It consists of 848 negatives, 3.5 x 3 cm, and 37 photographic prints, 23.9 x 30 cm and smaller.

The images chiefly document political and cultural turmoil surrounding the events chiefly in Bologna (561 images) and Rome (249 images), known as Movimento del '77, as well as similar events in Montalto di Castro and unidentified locations (38 images).
Contact prints have various images circled or crossed out in red pencil.
Not all images on the contact prints have extant negatives.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 18 Contact prints (over 500 images). 24 negatives attached to one of the contact prints. Approx. 500 negatives

did.unittitle: Francesco Lorusso, (the guy killed in Bologna, March 12, 1977) in the parade with military 1 contact printing (2 images), 4 negatives
Struggle Committee offsite 2 contact prints (70 images), 70 negatives
Roma 17 February 1977. Expulsion of Lama 2 contact prints (41 images), 36 negatives
Indian in assembly 1 contact print (3 images), 6 negatives
Indians and parties in the University of Rome 2 contact prints (66 images), 54 negatives
Indians and clashes at the University of Rome 3 contact prints (76 images), 82 negatives
Photographs of Montalto di Castro and Unidentified Locations
The capture and killing Muscio Maria Pia Vianale and Franca Salerno. Torture 1 contact print (18 images), 18 negatives

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photos appeared on the Manifesto of French intellectuals

did.unittitle: The turning point of the EUR 2 contact printings (5 images), 5 negatives
Montalto di Castro 1 contact print (1 image), 2 negatives

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains photographic prints documenting social and political movements in Italy from 1970 to 1985, with some dating in the 2000s. Included are images of protests, demonstrations, marches, strikes, pickets, occupations, and other public gatherings in locations such as Rome, Bologna, Milan, Florence, Marghera, Turin, and Palermo. A number of photographs depict automobile workers at Fiat's Mirafiori and Autobianchi factories, utility workers at Montedison, trade union members, students, and others involved in the labor movements. One group of photographs is focused on women activists.

There are also images documenting protests against the treatment of the poor, and related actions by left-wing groups Lotta Continua and Potere Operaio to address housing issues in Rome's urban zones Magliana, Casal Bruciato, and San Basilio. Some of the photographs feature people who were homeless, squatting, or facing eviction. The series further contains a group of photographs from the Southern Italian regions of Calabria, Sicily, and Sardinia.
Photographs are signed by D'Amico, with manuscript captions in Italian.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four groupings: Lavoro operai, Donne, Sud, and Guerra ai poveri. Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

descriptivenote.p: "Lavoro operai"
"Donne"
"Guerra ai poveri"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains 250 photographic prints and contact prints documenting the First Intifada (1987-1993) and Second Intifada (2000-2005) in Palestine. D'Amico focused primarily on the lives of civilians during the uprisings against Israel, with many images featuring children with their families at home, in school, and in public spaces like mosques and marketplaces in occupied Palestine. Photographs also depict acts of resistance by Palestinians, such as demonstrations, graffiti, barricades, and stone-throwing, and peace missions and protests by international activists. Other images portray checkpoints and the presence of Israel Defense Forces and police; funeral processions and surviving family members of the dead; and the aftermath of house demolitions and bombings. D'Amico also captured peaceful interactions between Palestinians and Israelis. Locations depicted include Khan Yunis in Gaza Strip, Hebron and Ramallah in the West Bank, and Jerusalem, as well as refugee camps such as Dheisheh and Kalandia.
Most photographs are signed by D'Amico, with manuscript captions in Italian.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in chronological order, with undated materials filed at the end of the sequence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains 250 photographic prints of protests in defense of the homeless population in Rome, dating from 2004 to 2019. Common subjects include homeless encampments, marches, street parades, and scenes of individuals and families. Several photographs are signed, with manuscript captions in Italian that indicate a mix of dates and cities, such as Bologna and Milan.

The series also includes 33 notebooks with autograph manuscript essay notes and one index notebook describing the contents of each notebook. Notebooks were labeled and arranged in numeric order by D'Amico. They document D'Amico's creative process and date from approximately 1980 to 2012, with the bulk of materials spanning the 1990s and early 2000s.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two groupings: Photographs, and Notebooks.

did.unittitle: Protests in defense of the homeless in Rome

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two groupings: Photographs, and Notebooks.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains 173 photographic prints and 50 contact prints documenting the living conditions of the Roma (or Romani) people in and around Rome from 1980 to 2010. Many images feature children and their families living in squatter settlements such as Casilino 700, one of the largest Roma encampments in Europe until its demolition in 2000. Photographs depict social gatherings, weddings, funerals, and protests by the Roma. D'Amico also documents state persecution of the Roma, including forced evictions by police. Locations depicted include the Casilino, Centocelle, and Garbatella neighborhoods of Rome. Photographs are signed by D'Amico, with manuscript captions in Italian.

The series also includes 35 notebooks with autograph manuscript essay notes and drafts of D'Amico's books Fotografia e destino: Appunti sull'immagine (2020) and Misericordia e tradimento: Fotografia, bellezza, verità (2021). Most notebooks were labeled and arranged in numeric order by D'Amico.

did.unittitle: Romani people in Rome, 50 contact prints
Romani people in Rome, 7 x 9.5" photographic prints
